old_claim.note: Supply Chain Management Software supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because a warehouse management system can be standalone or a component integrated into broader supply-chain-management software.

source_shape.source_locator: Warehouse management system overview and warehouse management process sections describing WMS-guided receiving, storage, picking, packing, and shipping.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes WMS as warehouse operations software for inventory, storage locations, picking, packing, and shipping.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That scope supports WMS as a warehouse execution system; it does not support making broader supply-chain-management software a prerequisite of WMS.

ontology_before: Warehouse management systems depended on broader supply-chain-management software, reversing a common component/suite relationship.
ontology_after: Warehouse management systems keep barcode automation and database systems as practical enablers while SCM software remains a separate broader logistics platform.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: The graph no longer models WMS as downstream of broader supply-chain-management software.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to a WMS module that exists only inside a specific SCM suite.
A source showed early WMS originated strictly as a dependent subsystem of SCM software.
A future integration node represented WMS-SCM suite integration rather than WMS itself.
short_reason: WMS is a warehouse execution system and can be standalone; broader SCM software is not a prerequisite.
